content.problemStatement: Suppose there is a X x Y x Z 3D matrix A of numbers having coordinates (i, j, k) where 0 ≤ i < X, 0 ≤ j < Y, 0 ≤ k < Z. Now another X x Y x Z matrix B is defined from A such that the (i, j, k) element of B is the sum of all the the numbers in A in the cuboid defined by the (0, 0, 0) and (i, j, k) elements as the diagonally opposite vertices. In other word (i, j, k) in B is the sum of numbers of A having coordinates (a, b, c) such that 0 ≤ a ≤ i, 0 ≤ b ≤ j, 0 ≤ c ≤ k. The problem is that given B, you have to find out A.
constraints: All elements in the matrix B are integers
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with a line containing three integers X, Y, and Z, representing the dimensions of the 3D matrix. This is followed by X*Y lines, each containing Z numbers, representing the elements of the matrix B.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the elements of the matrix A in the same format as the input matrix B.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved by using the concept of prefix sum. We can calculate the prefix sum of the 3D matrix B and then use it to find the elements of the matrix A. The key insight is that the (i, j, k) element of B is the sum of all the numbers in A in the cuboid defined by the (0, 0, 0) and (i, j, k) elements as the diagonally opposite vertices.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * X * Y * Z) per test case, O(T * X * Y * Z) total.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(X * Y * Z) — the dominant structure is the 3D matrix A.
